title: An ideal gas is at temperature $27^\circ\text{C}$. If the gas is heated such that its root mean square (RMS) speed of molecules is doubled, the final temperature of the gas will be:
notes: Remember to convert temperature to Kelvin: $T(\text{K}) = T(\text{C}) + 273$. RMS speed is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$.
solution_text: Initial temperature $T_1 = 27^\circ\text{C} = 27 + 273 = 300\,\text{K}$. The RMS speed is $v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}} \implies v_{\text{rms}} \propto \sqrt{T}$. If the RMS speed is doubled ($v_2 = 2v_1$), then the temperature must increase by a factor of 4: $T_2 = 4 T_1 = 4 \times 300 = 1200\,\text{K}$. Converting back to Celsius: $T_2 = 1200 - 273 = 927^\circ\text{C}$. Option D is correct.
common_mistake: Doubling the Celsius temperature directly, yielding $54^\circ\text{C}$ or $108^\circ\text{C}$, or forgetting to subtract 273 at the end.

title: An ideal gas undergoes two separate processes, one isothermal and the other adiabatic, from the same initial state $(P_1, V_1)$ to the same final volume $V_2$ ($V_2 > V_1$). If $W_{\text{iso}}$ and $W_{\text{adia}}$ are the work done in the isothermal and adiabatic processes respectively, then:
notes: Draw the PV diagrams for both processes. The isothermal curve is less steep than the adiabatic curve, so the area under the isothermal curve is larger.
solution_text: In a PV diagram, both processes start at the same point $(P_1, V_1)$ and end at $V_2$. The slope of the adiabatic curve is steeper ($dP/dV = -\gamma P/V$) than that of the isothermal curve ($dP/dV = -P/V$) since $\gamma > 1$. Therefore, for expansion ($V_2 > V_1$), the adiabatic curve lies below the isothermal curve. The work done is the area under the PV curve. Since the isothermal curve lies above the adiabatic curve, the area under the isothermal curve is larger: $W_{\text{iso}} > W_{\text{adia}}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming adiabatic work is larger because the temperature drops, or confusing expansion with compression (in compression, $W_{\text{adia}} > W_{\text{iso}}$).

title: A mixture contains 2 moles of Helium (monatomic, $C_v = \frac{3}{2}R$) and 3 moles of Nitrogen (diatomic, $C_v = \frac{5}{2}R$). The equivalent molar specific heat of the mixture at constant volume ($C_{v,\text{eq}}$) is:
notes: Use the mixture formula for specific heat: $C_{v,\text{eq}} = \frac{n_1 C_{v1} + n_2 C_{v2}}{n_1 + n_2}$.
solution_text: For Helium ($n_1 = 2$): $C_{v1} = 1.5R$. For Nitrogen ($n_2 = 3$): $C_{v2} = 2.5R$. The equivalent specific heat is $C_{v,\text{eq}} = \frac{2(1.5R) + 3(2.5R)}{2 + 3} = \frac{3R + 7.5R}{5} = \frac{10.5R}{5} = 2.1R$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Taking simple average of specific heats ($2.0R$) neglecting the mole fractions.

title: A Carnot engine operates between a source at temperature $400\,\text{K}$ and a sink at temperature $300\,\text{K}$. If the engine absorbs $1200\,\text{J}$ of heat from the source per cycle, the work done by the engine per cycle is ________ $\text{J}$.
solution_text: Efficiency of a Carnot engine is $\eta = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{sink}}}{T_{\text{source}}} = 1 - \frac{300}{400} = 0.25$ or $25\%$. We also know $\eta = \frac{W}{Q_{\text{in}}} \implies W = \eta \cdot Q_{\text{in}} = 0.25 \times 1200 = 300\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Using efficiency as $\eta = T_2 / T_1 = 75\%$ and calculating $W = 900\,\text{J}$.

title: The mean free path of gas molecules of diameter $d$ at a number density $n$ is proportional to:
notes: The exact formula for mean free path is $\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi n d^2}$.
solution_text: The mean free path $\lambda$ represents the average distance travelled by a molecule between successive collisions. It is given by the formula $\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi n d^2}$, where $n$ is the number density of molecules and $d$ is their collision diameter. Therefore, $\lambda \propto \frac{1}{n d^2}$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking that mean free path is inversely proportional to $d$ instead of $d^2$ (collision cross section is proportional to $\pi d^2$).

title: Two rods of same length and area of cross-section but of thermal conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$ are connected in series. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the combination is:
notes: Thermal resistance in series: $R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$. Use $R = L/(KA)$. Here equivalent length is $2L$.
solution_text: Let length of each rod be $L$ and area be $A$. Thermal resistance of rod 1: $R_1 = \frac{L}{K_1 A}$. Resistance of rod 2: $R_2 = \frac{L}{K_2 A}$. Since they are connected in series: $R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 = \frac{L}{A}\left(\frac{1}{K_1} + \frac{1}{K_2}\right) = \frac{L}{A}\left(\frac{K_1 + K_2}{K_1 K_2}\right)$. For the combination, total length is $2L$ and area is $A$, so $R_{\text{eq}} = \frac{2L}{K_{\text{eq}} A}$. Equating them: $\frac{2L}{K_{\text{eq}} A} = \frac{L}{A}\left(\frac{K_1 + K_2}{K_1 K_2}\right) \implies \frac{2}{K_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{K_1 + K_2}{K_1 K_2} \implies K_{\text{eq}} = \frac{2K_1 K_2}{K_1 + K_2}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Adding the conductivities directly (which corresponds to parallel connection) or using parallel resistance rules.

title: During a thermodynamic process, a system absorbs $100\,\text{J}$ of heat and performs $40\,\text{J}$ of work. If the initial internal energy of the system was $50\,\text{J}$, the final internal energy of the system is ________ $\text{J}$.
solution_text: By First Law of Thermodynamics: $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W \implies 100 = \Delta U + 40 \implies \Delta U = 60\,\text{J}$. Since $\Delta U = U_f - U_i \implies 60 = U_f - 50 \implies U_f = 110\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Subtracting heat absorbed or adding work incorrectly, violating the sign conventions of the First Law.

title: An ideal gas is taken through a cyclic process ABCDA where A is $(P_0, V_0)$, B is $(2P_0, V_0)$, C is $(2P_0, 3V_0)$, and D is $(P_0, 3V_0)$ on a PV diagram. The net work done by the gas in one complete cycle is ________ $P_0 V_0$.
solution_text: The cyclic process forms a rectangle on the PV diagram. The vertices are $(P_0, V_0)$, $(2P_0, V_0)$, $(2P_0, 3V_0)$, and $(P_0, 3V_0)$. The work done in a cyclic process is equal to the area enclosed by the cycle on the PV diagram. Area of rectangle $= \text{height} \times \text{width} = (2P_0 - P_0) \times (3V_0 - V_0) = P_0 \times 2V_0 = 2 P_0 V_0$. Since the cycle is clockwise, the work done is positive: $W = 2 P_0 V_0$. Thus the coefficient is 2.
common_mistake: Calculating work done for each path individually and making summation errors, or using negative sign (clockwise cycles are positive work on PV diagrams).

title: One mole of an ideal monatomic gas ($C_v = 1.5R$) undergoes a polytropic process described by $P V^2 = \text{constant}$. The molar heat capacity $C$ of the gas during this process is:
notes: For a polytropic process $P V^n = \text{constant}$, the molar heat capacity is given by $C = C_v + \frac{R}{1-n}$. Here $n=2$. Let's adjust options to make option A = $R/2$ and option B = $-R/2$. Let's check: $C = 1.5R + R/(1-2) = 1.5R - R = 0.5R$. Wait, so $C = 0.5R$. Yes. Let's make options A: $0.5R$, B: $R$, C: $1.5R$, D: $2.5R$ and set A as correct.
solution_text: For a polytropic process $PV^n = \text{constant}$, the work done is $W = \frac{R\Delta T}{1-n}$. By First Law of Thermodynamics, $Q = \Delta U + W \implies C \Delta T = C_v \Delta T + \frac{R \Delta T}{1-n} \implies C = C_v + \frac{R}{1-n}$. Here, the process is $PV^2 = \text{constant}$, so $n = 2$. For a monatomic gas, $C_v = 1.5R$. Thus: $C = 1.5R + \frac{R}{1-2} = 1.5R - R = 0.5R$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using the wrong formula for polytropic work, or confusing the exponent $n$ (putting $n=-2$ instead of $n=2$).

title: A refrigerator is working between ice point ($0^\circ\text{C}$) and room temperature ($27^\circ\text{C}$). To remove $90\,\text{J}$ of heat from the refrigerator per second, the power required to run the compressor is ________ $\text{W}$.
solution_text: The coefficient of performance (COP) of a Carnot refrigerator is $\beta = \frac{T_{\text{sink}}}{T_{\text{source}} - T_{\text{sink}}}$. Here $T_{\text{sink}} = 0^\circ\text{C} = 273\,\text{K}$, and $T_{\text{source}} = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300\,\text{K}$. Thus, $\beta = \frac{273}{300 - 273} = \frac{273}{27} = 10.11$. By definition, $\beta = \frac{Q_{\text{sink}}}{W} \implies W = \frac{Q_{\text{sink}}}{\beta}$. In terms of rate: Power $P = \frac{dW}{dt} = \frac{1}{\beta} \frac{dQ_{\text{sink}}}{dt} = \frac{90}{10.11} \approx 8.9\,\text{W}$. Rounding to the nearest integer, the power required is 9 W.
common_mistake: Using Celsius temperatures directly in COP calculations (which would yield $0/27 = 0$ power required, a physical impossibility).

title: A diatomic gas molecule has $5$ degrees of freedom at room temperature. The total internal energy of $2\,\text{moles}$ of this gas at temperature $T$ is:
notes: By the law of equipartition of energy, internal energy per mole is $U = \frac{f}{2}RT$. For $n$ moles, $U = n\frac{f}{2}RT$.
solution_text: The internal energy of $n$ moles of a gas with $f$ degrees of freedom is $U = n \left(\frac{f}{2}RT\right)$. Given $n = 2$ and $f = 5$ (for a diatomic gas at room temperature): $U = 2 \left(\frac{5}{2}RT\right) = 5RT$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using $f=3$ (monatomic degrees of freedom) or forgetting the factor of $1/2$.

title: An ideal gas at pressure $P$ is compressed adiabatically to $1/8$ of its initial volume. If the ratio of specific heats of the gas is $\gamma = 1.67$ (monatomic), the final pressure of the gas is ________ $P$.
solution_text: For an adiabatic process: $P V^\gamma = \text{constant} \implies P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma \implies P_2 = P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^\gamma$. Given $V_2 = V_1 / 8$ and $\gamma = 5/3 \approx 1.67$: $P_2 = P \left(8\right)^{5/3} = P (2^3)^{5/3} = P (2)^5 = 32P$. Hence, the final pressure is 32 P.
common_mistake: Using Boyle's Law $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (yielding $8P$) which only holds for isothermal processes.

title: A hot liquid cools from $70^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$ in $5\,\text{minutes}$ in a room of temperature $30^\circ\text{C}$. The time taken by the liquid to cool from $60^\circ\text{C}$ to $50^\circ\text{C}$ in the same room is:

options.D: Depends on the density of the liquid

notes: According to Newton's Law of Cooling, rate of cooling decreases as the temperature of the body approaches the surrounding temperature.
solution_text: According to Newton's Law of Cooling, the rate of cooling is proportional to the temperature difference between the body and the surroundings: $-\frac{dT}{dt} = K(T - T_s)$. As the liquid cools, its temperature $T$ decreases, so the temperature difference $(T - T_s)$ decreases. Consequently, the rate of cooling decreases. Thus, it takes more time to cool through the same temperature interval ($60^\circ\text{C}$ to $50^\circ\text{C}$) than the initial one ($70^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$). Hence, the time taken must be more than 5 minutes. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming linear cooling rates (which would mean exactly 5 minutes).

title: A spherical black body of radius $12\,\text{cm}$ radiates $450\,\text{W}$ power at $500\,\text{K}$. If the radius is halved and the temperature is doubled, the power radiated will be:
notes: Stefan-Boltzmann Law: $P = \sigma A T^4 = \sigma (4\pi r^2) T^4 \implies P \propto r^2 T^4$.
solution_text: By Stefan-Boltzmann Law, the power radiated by a black body is $P = \sigma A T^4$. For a sphere, $A = 4\pi r^2 \implies P \propto r^2 T^4$. The ratio of final to initial power is: $\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2 \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \left(2\right)^4 = \frac{1}{4} \times 16 = 4$. Given $P_1 = 450\,\text{W}$, the final power is $P_2 = 4 \times 450 = 1800\,\text{W}$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Forgetting that surface area of a sphere is proportional to $r^2$ rather than $r$, or using $P \propto T$ instead of $T^4$.

title: The mass of ice at $0^\circ\text{C}$ that must be mixed with $100\,\text{g}$ of water at $80^\circ\text{C}$ in a calorimeter of negligible heat capacity so that the final temperature of the mixture is $20^\circ\text{C}$, is ________ $\text{g}$. (Take latent heat of fusion of ice $= 80\,\text{cal/g}$ and specific heat of water $= 1\,\text{cal/g}^\circ\text{C}$).
solution_text: Let $m$ be the mass of ice. Heat gained by ice to melt and then reach $20^\circ\text{C}$ is: $Q_{\text{gained}} = m L_f + m c_w (20 - 0) = m(80) + m(1)(20) = 100m\,\text{cal}$. Heat lost by hot water cooling from $80^\circ\text{C}$ to $20^\circ\text{C}$ is: $Q_{\text{lost}} = m_w c_w (80 - 20) = 100 \times 1 \times 60 = 6000\,\text{cal}$. By calorimetry principle, $Q_{\text{gained}} = Q_{\text{lost}} \implies 100m = 6000 \implies m = 60\,\text{g}$.
common_mistake: Forgetting the heat required to raise the melted ice water from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $20^\circ\text{C}$, which leads to $m = 75\,\text{g}$.

title: A calorimeter of water equivalent $10\,\text{g}$ contains $90\,\text{g}$ of water at $24^\circ\text{C}$. When steam at $100^\circ\text{C}$ is passed through it, the state of the system becomes water at $80^\circ\text{C}$. The mass of steam condensed is ________ $\text{g}$. (Take latent heat of vaporization of steam $= 540\,\text{cal/g}$ and specific heat of water $= 1\,\text{cal/g}^\circ\text{C}$)
notes: Heat gained by calorimeter and water is equal to heat lost by steam during condensation and cooling.
solution_text:
Water equivalent of calorimeter $W = 10\,\text{g}$. Mass of water $m_w = 90\,\text{g}$. Initial temperature $T_i = 24^\circ\text{C}$. Final temperature $T_f = 80^\circ\text{C}$.
Heat gained by calorimeter and water:
$Q_{\text{gained}} = (m_w + W) \cdot c_w \cdot (T_f - T_i) = (90 + 10) \times 1 \times (80 - 24) = 100 \times 56 = 5600\,\text{cal}$.
Heat lost by steam of mass $m$:
$Q_{\text{lost}} = m L_v + m c_w (100 - T_f) = m(540) + m(1)(100 - 80) = 540m + 20m = 560m\,\text{cal}$.
By calorimetry principle: $Q_{\text{gained}} = Q_{\text{lost}} \implies 5600 = 560m \implies m = 10\,\text{g}$.
common_mistake: Forgetting to include the water equivalent of the calorimeter, or neglecting the heat lost by condensed steam as it cools from $100^\circ\text{C}$ to $80^\circ\text{C}$.

title: The ratio of thermal conductivity of copper to that of brass is $4:1$. Two identical rods of copper and brass are joined end-to-end in series. If the free end of the copper rod is at $100^\circ\text{C}$ and the free end of the brass rod is at $0^\circ\text{C}$, the temperature of the junction in steady state is ________ $^\circ\text{C}$.
notes: In steady state, the rate of heat flow through copper rod must equal the rate of heat flow through brass rod.
solution_text: Let the temperature of the junction be $T_j$. In steady state, the rate of heat flow through both rods must be equal: $H_{\text{copper}} = H_{\text{brass}} \implies \frac{K_{\text{Cu}} A (100 - T_j)}{L} = \frac{K_{\text{brass}} A (T_j - 0)}{L}$. Since the rods are identical, $A$ and $L$ cancel: $K_{\text{Cu}} (100 - T_j) = K_{\text{brass}} T_j \implies \frac{K_{\text{Cu}}}{K_{\text{brass}}} (100 - T_j) = T_j$. Given $\frac{K_{\text{Cu}}}{K_{\text{brass}}} = 4 \implies 4(100 - T_j) = T_j \implies 400 - 4T_j = T_j \implies 5T_j = 400 \implies T_j = 80^\circ\text{C}$.
common_mistake: Setting up the heat flow equations with the wrong direction of temperature differences, or using the wrong ratio $K_{\text{brass}}/K_{\text{Cu}} = 4$.

title: A black body radiates maximum energy at a wavelength of $\lambda_0$ when its temperature is $T$. If the temperature of the black body is increased to $2T$, the wavelength at which it radiates maximum energy will be:
notes: Apply Wien's Displacement Law.
solution_text: According to Wien's Displacement Law, the wavelength $\lambda_{\text{max}}$ corresponding to maximum energy emission is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature $T$: $\lambda_{\text{max}} \cdot T = b \text{ (constant)}$. Thus, $\lambda_1 T_1 = \lambda_2 T_2 \implies \lambda_0 T = \lambda_2 (2T) \implies \lambda_2 = \lambda_0 / 2$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing Wien's law with Stefan's law and thinking the wavelength scales as $1/T^4$, leading to $\lambda_0/16$.

title: A spherical shell of inner radius $R_1$ and outer radius $R_2$ is made of a material of thermal conductivity $K$. The inner and outer surfaces are maintained at constant temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ ($T_1 > T_2$) respectively. The rate of heat flow through the shell is proportional to:
notes: Find the thermal resistance of a thin spherical element and integrate over the shell thickness.
solution_text:
Consider a thin spherical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. The thermal resistance of this shell is $dR = \frac{dr}{K (4\pi r^2)}$. The total thermal resistance of the spherical shell is obtained by integrating from $R_1$ to $R_2$:
$R_{\text{total}} = \int_{R_1}^{R_2} \frac{dr}{4\pi K r^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi K} \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_{R_1}^{R_2} = \frac{R_2 - R_1}{4\pi K R_1 R_2}$.
Rate of heat flow is $Q = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{4\pi K R_1 R_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{R_2 - R_1}$.
Therefore, the rate of heat flow is proportional to $\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_2 - R_1}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using a flat wall approximation where resistance is $\frac{R_2 - R_1}{K(4\pi R_1^2)}$, ignoring the change of surface area with radius.

title: The temperature at which the root mean square (RMS) speed of Helium atoms ($M = 4\,\text{g/mol}$) equals the escape velocity from the Earth's surface ($v_{\text{esc}} = 11.2\,\text{km/s}$) is approximately ________ $\times 10^4\,\text{K}$. (Take $R = 8.3\,\text{J/mol\,K}$, and round to the nearest integer)
notes: Set the formula for RMS speed equal to the escape velocity and solve for temperature.
solution_text:
Escape velocity $v_{\text{esc}} = 11.2\,\text{km/s} = 1.12 \times 10^4\,\text{m/s}$. Molar mass of Helium $M = 4\,\text{g/mol} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol}$. RMS speed formula is $v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}$. Setting $v_{\text{rms}} = v_{\text{esc}} \implies v_{\text{esc}}^2 = \frac{3RT}{M} \implies T = \frac{M v_{\text{esc}}^2}{3R}$. Substituting the values:
$T = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times (1.12 \times 10^4)^2}{3 \times 8.3} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2544 \times 10^8}{24.9} = \frac{5.0176 \times 10^5}{24.9} \approx 20151\,\text{K} \approx 2 \times 10^4\,\text{K}$. Thus, the coefficient is 2.
common_mistake: Using Helium molar mass as $4\,\text{kg/mol}$ instead of $4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{kg/mol}$, or forgetting to convert escape velocity to m/s.

title: A vessel contains $14\,\text{g}$ of Nitrogen gas ($N_2$, molar mass $28\,\text{g/mol}$) and $16\,\text{g}$ of Oxygen gas ($O_2$, molar mass $32\,\text{g/mol}$) at temperature $T = 300\,\text{K}$. The total internal energy of the mixture is ________ $R$. (Take both gases to be ideal diatomic gases with $5$ degrees of freedom, neglect vibrational modes)
notes: Calculate the number of moles for each gas, then apply the internal energy formula $U = n \frac{f}{2} R T$.
solution_text: Moles of $N_2$, $n_1 = \frac{14}{28} = 0.5\,\text{mol}$. Moles of $O_2$, $n_2 = \frac{16}{32} = 0.5\,\text{mol}$. Both gases are diatomic and at room temperature, so they have $f=5$ degrees of freedom. The internal energy of the mixture is: $U = U_1 + U_2 = n_1 \frac{5}{2}RT + n_2 \frac{5}{2}RT = (n_1 + n_2) \frac{5}{2}RT = (0.5 + 0.5) \frac{5}{2}R(300) = 1 \times 2.5 \times 300 R = 750R$. Therefore, the total internal energy in units of $R$ is 750.
common_mistake: Using $f=3$ (monatomic) instead of $f=5$ (diatomic), or adding the masses together directly as total moles.

title: If the pressure of a gas in a closed container of constant volume is doubled by heating, the mean free path of the gas molecules will change by a factor of ________.
notes: Recall the dependency of mean free path on the number density of the gas molecules.
solution_text: The mean free path is given by the formula $\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}\pi n d^2}$, where $n = N/V$ is the number density of the gas molecules and $d$ is the molecular diameter. Since the container is closed and has constant volume, both the total number of molecules $N$ and the volume $V$ remain constant. Therefore, the number density $n$ is constant. Consequently, the mean free path $\lambda$ does not change (it changes by a factor of 1) even though the pressure is doubled by increasing the temperature.
common_mistake: Assuming that since pressure is doubled, the density is doubled and thus the mean free path is halved, neglecting that volume is constant.

title: At a given temperature, the average translational kinetic energy of a molecule of Helium (monatomic) and a molecule of Carbon Dioxide (triatomic) are $K_{\text{He}}$ and $K_{\text{CO}_2}$ respectively. The ratio $K_{\text{He}} / K_{\text{CO}_2}$ is:
notes: Differentiate between translational kinetic energy and total kinetic energy.
solution_text: According to the kinetic theory of gases, the average translational kinetic energy of a single molecule of any gas depends only on the absolute temperature $T$ and is given by $K_{\text{trans}} = \frac{3}{2} k_B T$, regardless of the molecular weight or atomicity (degrees of freedom only affect the total kinetic energy, not the translational kinetic energy). Since both gases are at the same temperature, their average translational kinetic energies are equal. Thus, the ratio is 1:1. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using the total kinetic energy which includes rotational and vibrational modes, leading to 3:6 (or 1:2), or using the ratio of molecular masses.

title: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution curve for an ideal gas when its temperature is increased?

options.A: The peak shifts to the right and becomes taller.
options.B: The peak shifts to the left and becomes broader.
options.C: The peak shifts to the right and becomes broader and shorter.
options.D: The peak remains at the same speed but the area under the curve increases.

notes: Consider the relationship between temperature, most probable speed, and the normalization of the distribution curve.
solution_text: As temperature increases, the most probable speed $v_{\text{mp}} = \sqrt{\frac{2RT}{M}}$ increases, so the peak of the distribution shifts to the right (higher speed). Since the total number of molecules (area under the curve) must remain constant, and the distribution spreads out over a wider range of speeds (becoming broader), the height of the peak must decrease (become shorter). Therefore, the peak shifts to the right and becomes broader and shorter. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking the peak gets taller because higher temperature means higher speeds, neglecting the constraint that the area under the curve must remain constant.

title: An ideal gas at initial temperature $T$ is compressed adiabatically to $1/16$ of its initial volume. If the ratio of specific heats of the gas is $\gamma = 1.5$, the final temperature of the gas is $k T$. The value of $k$ is ________.
notes: Use the adiabatic relation between temperature and volume: $T V^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$.
solution_text:
For an adiabatic process, the relation between temperature and volume is $T V^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$. Let the initial state be $(T_1, V_1) = (T, V)$ and the final state be $(T_2, V_2) = (kT, V/16)$. We have:
$T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1} = T_2 V_2^{\gamma-1} \implies T V^{1.5-1} = (kT) \left(\frac{V}{16}\right)^{1.5-1} \implies V^{0.5} = k \left(\frac{V}{16}\right)^{0.5} \implies 1 = k \frac{1}{\sqrt{16}} \implies 1 = \frac{k}{4} \implies k = 4$. Thus, the final temperature is $4T$, so $k=4$.
common_mistake: Using the wrong exponent $\gamma$ instead of $\gamma - 1$, which would give $k = 16^{1.5} = 64$.

title: An ideal gas is taken through a closed cyclic thermodynamic process $A \to B \to C \to A$. If the heat supplied to the system during the cycle is $50\,\text{J}$, the net change in the internal energy of the gas over one complete cycle is ________ $\text{J}$.
notes: State functions depend only on the state itself, not on the path.
solution_text: Internal energy $U$ is a state function, which means its value depends only on the current state of the system (characterized by pressure, volume, temperature) and is independent of the path taken to reach that state. In a complete cyclic process, the system starts and ends at the exact same state. Therefore, the net change in internal energy over a complete cycle is $\Delta U = U_f - U_i = 0\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Applying $\Delta U = Q = 50\,\text{J}$, failing to recognize that for a cycle, work is also done and internal energy returns to its original value.

title: When a monatomic ideal gas is heated at constant pressure, the fraction of heat energy supplied that goes to increase the internal energy of the gas is:
notes: Compute the ratio of change in internal energy to heat supplied at constant pressure: $\Delta U / Q_p = C_v / C_p$.
solution_text:
At constant pressure (isobaric process), the heat supplied is $Q = n C_p \Delta T$. The change in internal energy is $\Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$. The fraction of heat that increases internal energy is:
$\frac{\Delta U}{Q} = \frac{C_v}{C_p} = \frac{1}{\gamma}$.
For a monatomic gas, $C_v = \frac{3}{2}R$, $C_p = \frac{5}{2}R$, so $\gamma = 5/3$. Thus, the fraction is:
$\frac{\Delta U}{Q} = \frac{3/2 R}{5/2 R} = \frac{3}{5}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using diatomic gas values ($\gamma = 7/5$), which would give $5/7$ (option D).

title: An ideal gas undergoes a thermodynamic process where its pressure $P$ and volume $V$ are related by $P V^3 = \text{constant}$. If the gas is diatomic ($C_v = 2.5R$), the molar heat capacity $C$ of the gas during this process is:
notes: Use the formula for molar heat capacity of a polytropic process: $C = C_v + \frac{R}{1-n}$.
solution_text:
For a polytropic process $PV^n = \text{constant}$, the molar heat capacity is given by $C = C_v + \frac{R}{1-n}$.
Here, the process is $PV^3 = \text{constant}$, so $n = 3$.
The gas is diatomic, so its molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_v = 2.5R$.
Substituting these values:
$C = 2.5R + \frac{R}{1-3} = 2.5R - 0.5R = 2.0R$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Adding $0.5R$ instead of subtracting it, leading to $3.0R$ (option C).

title: An ideal gas undergoes a cyclic process represented by a circle on the $P-V$ diagram. If the circle has a pressure range from $1\,\text{bar}$ to $3\,\text{bar}$ and a volume range from $1\,\text{litre}$ to $3\,\text{litre}$, the net work done by the gas in one clockwise cycle is ________ $\pi\,\text{J}$. (Take $1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa}$ and $1\,\text{litre} = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$)
notes: The area of the circle/ellipse on a PV plot is given by $\pi a b$ where $a$ and $b$ are the semi-axes.
solution_text: The cyclic path on the $P-V$ diagram is an ellipse with axes parallel to the pressure and volume axes. The semi-axis along volume is $a = \frac{V_{\text{max}} - V_{\text{min}}}{2} = \frac{3\,\text{L} - 1\,\text{L}}{2} = 1\,\text{L} = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$. The semi-axis along pressure is $b = \frac{P_{\text{max}} - P_{\text{min}}}{2} = \frac{3\,\text{bar} - 1\,\text{bar}}{2} = 1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{N/m}^2$. The area of this ellipse is $W = \pi a b = \pi \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3 \times 10^5\,\text{N/m}^2 = 100\pi\,\text{J}$. Since the cycle is clockwise, the net work done is positive. Thus, in units of $\pi\,\text{J}$, the work done is 100.
common_mistake: Calculating area using diameters instead of semi-axes, or failing to convert units.

title: One mole of an ideal gas at temperature $T$ expands isothermally from an initial volume $V$ to a final volume $2V$. The work done by the gas during this process is:
notes: Integrate $P dV$ using $P = RT/V$ for 1 mole of ideal gas.
solution_text: For an isothermal expansion of 1 mole of an ideal gas at temperature $T$, the work done is given by: $W = \int_{V_i}^{V_f} P dV = \int_V^{2V} \frac{RT}{V} dV = RT \ln\left(\frac{2V}{V}\right) = RT \ln 2$. Since it is an expansion ($V_f > V_i$), work is done by the gas, so it is positive. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Choosing the negative sign (option B), which represents work done on the gas.

title: An ideal gas undergoes three different expansion processes from the same initial state $(P_0, V_0)$ to the same final volume $2V_0$: (1) isobaric, (2) isothermal, and (3) adiabatic. If $W_1$, $W_2$, and $W_3$ are the work done in these processes respectively, then:
notes: Represent the processes on a PV diagram and compare the areas under the curves.
solution_text:
On a $P-V$ diagram, the work done in a process is represented by the area under the process curve.
1. Isobaric process: Pressure remains constant at $P_0$. The curve is a horizontal line.
2. Isothermal process: Pressure decreases as $P = P_0 (V_0/V)$.
3. Adiabatic process: Pressure decreases more steeply as $P = P_0 (V_0/V)^\gamma$ where $\gamma > 1$.
Thus, for any volume $V > V_0$, the pressure is highest in the isobaric process, followed by the isothermal process, and lowest in the adiabatic process. Therefore, the area under the curves (work done) follows the order: $W_1 > W_2 > W_3$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing expansion with compression, where the order of work magnitude is reversed.

title: An ideal gas is taken through a cyclic process consisting of an isobaric expansion, an isochoric cooling, and an isothermal compression. On a temperature-volume ($T-V$) diagram, this cycle will look like:

options.A: A straight line passing through the origin, a horizontal line, and a curve of the form $T = \text{const}/V$
options.B: A straight line passing through the origin, a horizontal line, and a curve of the form $T = \text{const}$
options.C: A straight line passing through the origin, a vertical line, and a horizontal line
options.D: A horizontal line, a vertical line, and a straight line passing through the origin

notes: Translate the physical condition of each process to the relation between $T$ and $V$.
solution_text:
Let's analyze each process on a $T-V$ diagram:
1. Isobaric process: $P = \text{constant}$. From the ideal gas law $PV = nRT \implies T = \left(\frac{P}{nR}\right) V$. This is a straight line passing through the origin.
2. Isochoric process: $V = \text{constant}$. On a $T-V$ plot, constant volume corresponds to a vertical line.
3. Isothermal process: $T = \text{constant}$. On a $T-V$ plot, constant temperature corresponds to a horizontal line.
Combining these three, the cyclic process on a $T-V$ diagram is enclosed by a straight line passing through the origin, a vertical line, and a horizontal line. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming the isobaric process is a horizontal line on $T-V$, which is only true on a $P-V$ diagram.

title: One mole of an ideal gas is taken through a cyclic process $A \to C \to B \to A$ where $A = (P_0, V_0)$, $C = (2P_0, V_0)$, and $B = (P_0, 2V_0)$ on a $P-V$ diagram. The path $C \to B$ is a straight line. The net work done during the cycle is ________ $P_0 V_0$.
notes: Find the area enclosed by the triangle on the PV diagram, making sure to verify the sign of work based on cycle direction.
solution_text:
The vertices of the cycle are $A(P_0, V_0)$, $C(2P_0, V_0)$, and $B(P_0, 2V_0)$. This forms a right-angled triangle on the $P-V$ diagram.
- Path $A \to C$: Isochoric pressure increase ($V = V_0$, $W_{AC} = 0$).
- Path $C \to B$: Linear expansion from $(V_0, 2P_0)$ to $(2V_0, P_0)$. The work done is the area under the line: $W_{CB} = \frac{1}{2}(2P_0 + P_0)(2V_0 - V_0) = 1.5 P_0 V_0$.
- Path $B \to A$: Isobaric compression at $P = P_0$ from $2V_0$ to $V_0$. Work done: $W_{BA} = P_0 (V_0 - 2V_0) = -P_0 V_0$.
- Net work done: $W_{\text{net}} = W_{AC} + W_{CB} + W_{BA} = 0 + 1.5 P_0 V_0 - P_0 V_0 = 0.5 P_0 V_0$.
Alternatively, the area enclosed by the triangle is $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times (2V_0 - V_0) \times (2P_0 - P_0) = 0.5 P_0 V_0$. Since the cycle goes clockwise, the net work is positive: $0.5 P_0 V_0$. So the coefficient is 0.5.
common_mistake: Adding the path works incorrectly, or neglecting the factor of $1/2$ for the triangular area.

title: An ideal gas with specific heat ratio $\gamma$ undergoes an adiabatic process from initial state ($P_1, V_1, T_1$) to final state ($P_2, V_2, T_2$). The work done by the gas is given by the expression:
notes: Integrate $P dV$ using $P V^\gamma = \text{constant}$ to find the adiabatic work expression.
solution_text:
For an adiabatic process, $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$. The work done is:
$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{C}{V^\gamma} dV = \frac{C}{1-\gamma} [V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma}]$.
Since $C = P_1 V_1^\gamma = P_2 V_2^\gamma$, we substitute $C$:
$W = \frac{P_2 V_2^\gamma V_2^{1-\gamma} - P_1 V_1^\gamma V_1^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_1 V_1}{1-\gamma} = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing the sign in the denominator or numerator, leading to options B or C.

title: One mole of a real gas obeying the van der Waals equation of state: $\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT$ expands isothermally at temperature $T$ from volume $V_1$ to $V_2$. The work done by the gas during this expansion is:
notes: Rearrange the equation of state to express $P$ as a function of $V$, then perform integration $W = \int P dV$.
solution_text:
From the van der Waals equation: $P = \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2}$.
Work done during isothermal expansion is: $W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \left( \frac{RT}{V-b} - \frac{a}{V^2} \right) dV$.
Integrating each term:
$\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{RT}{V-b} dV = RT \ln(V-b) \Big|_{V_1}^{V_2} = RT \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right)$.
$-\int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{a}{V^2} dV = a \left[ \frac{1}{V} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} = a \left(\frac{1}{V_2} - \frac{1}{V_1}\right)$.
Adding the two terms gives: $W = RT \ln\left(\frac{V_2 - b}{V_1 - b}\right) + a\left(\frac{1}{V_2} - \frac{1}{V_1}\right)$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Integrating the term $-a/V^2$ with a sign error, resulting in a minus sign in front of the second term (option C).

title: For a gas with $f$ degrees of freedom, the ratio of specific heats $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is given by the expression:
notes: Recall $C_v$ and $C_p$ in terms of degrees of freedom $f$.
solution_text:
Molar specific heat at constant volume is $C_v = \frac{f}{2}R$. Molar specific heat at constant pressure is $C_p = C_v + R = \left(\frac{f}{2} + 1\right) R = \frac{f+2}{2} R$. The ratio of specific heats is:
$\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v} = \frac{\frac{f+2}{2}R}{\frac{f}{2}R} = \frac{f+2}{f} = 1 + \frac{2}{f}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing $C_p$ and $C_v$ expressions, leading to incorrect fractions.

title: A gas mixture consists of $2\,\text{moles}$ of Helium (monatomic, $C_v = 1.5R$) and $2\,\text{moles}$ of Nitrogen (diatomic, $C_v = 2.5R$). The effective specific heat ratio $\gamma_{\text{eq}}$ for the mixture is ________. (Multiply your answer by 10 for integer fill)
notes: Find the equivalent $C_v$ and $C_p$ values for the mixture, then take their ratio.
solution_text:
For Helium ($n_1 = 2$, monatomic): $C_{v1} = 1.5R, C_{p1} = 2.5R$.
For Nitrogen ($n_2 = 2$, diatomic): $C_{v2} = 2.5R, C_{p2} = 3.5R$.
The effective constant-volume specific heat of the mixture is:
$C_{v,\text{eq}} = \frac{n_1 C_{v1} + n_2 C_{v2}}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{2(1.5R) + 2(2.5R)}{2+2} = \frac{3R + 5R}{4} = 2R$.
The effective constant-pressure specific heat of the mixture is:
$C_{p,\text{eq}} = C_{v,\text{eq}} + R = 3R$.
The effective specific heat ratio is:
$\gamma_{\text{eq}} = \frac{C_{p,\text{eq}}}{C_{v,\text{eq}}} = \frac{3R}{2R} = 1.5$.
Multiplying by 10 gives 15.
common_mistake: Averaging the $\gamma$ values directly, which would give $(1.67 + 1.40)/2 = 1.535$, leading to 15 after rounding, but it is physically incorrect.

title: For any ideal gas, the difference between the molar heat capacity at constant pressure and at constant volume ($C_p - C_v$) is equal to ________ $R$. (Write the coefficient of $R$ as an integer)
notes: This relation is known as Mayer's relation.
solution_text: Mayer's relation states that for any ideal gas, the molar heat capacity at constant pressure ($C_p$) and the molar heat capacity at constant volume ($C_v$) are related by: $C_p - C_v = R$. Thus, the coefficient of $R$ is 1.
common_mistake: Using specific heat capacity per unit mass instead of molar heat capacity, which would make the difference $r$ (gas constant for that gas).

title: One mole of Helium (monatomic) is mixed with one mole of Oxygen (diatomic). The molar specific heat at constant pressure ($C_p$) of the mixture is:
notes: Find the average $C_p$ of the mixture since the moles of both gases are equal.
solution_text:
Molar specific heat of monatomic gas (Helium) at constant pressure is $C_{p1} = 2.5R$. Molar specific heat of diatomic gas (Oxygen) at constant pressure is $C_{p2} = 3.5R$.
For a mixture of equal moles ($n_1 = 1, n_2 = 1$):
$C_{p,\text{eq}} = \frac{n_1 C_{p1} + n_2 C_{p2}}{n_1 + n_2} = \frac{1(2.5R) + 1(3.5R)}{2} = 3R$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $C_v$ instead of $C_p$, resulting in $2R$ (which is not in the options).

title: A non-linear triatomic gas (like $H_2O$) has ________ degrees of freedom at room temperature, neglecting vibrational modes.
notes: Determine the translational and rotational degrees of freedom for a non-linear molecule.
solution_text: A non-linear triatomic molecule has 3 translational degrees of freedom (along x, y, and z axes) and 3 rotational degrees of freedom (rotation about three independent perpendicular axes). Since vibrational modes are neglected at room temperature, the total degrees of freedom is $3 + 3 = 6$.
common_mistake: Using 5 degrees of freedom, which is for linear diatomic/triatomic molecules.

title: A gas is found to have a specific heat ratio $\gamma = 1.33$ at room temperature. The gas molecules are most likely:

options.D: Linear diatomic with vibrational modes active

notes: Relate $\gamma$ to the degrees of freedom $f$ using $\gamma = 1 + 2/f$.
solution_text:
The ratio of specific heats is $\gamma = 1 + \frac{2}{f}$.
Given $\gamma = 1.33 \approx 4/3$:
$4/3 = 1 + 2/f \implies 2/f = 1/3 \implies f = 6$.
A degrees of freedom value of 6 at room temperature corresponds to a non-linear triatomic gas. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Confusing $\gamma = 1.33$ with $1.40$ (diatomic) or $1.67$ (monatomic).

title: An ideal monatomic gas ($C_v = 1.5R$) is taken through a process in which its pressure is directly proportional to its volume ($P \propto V$). The molar specific heat capacity of the gas in this process is:
notes: Find the polytropic exponent $n$ from the relation $P \propto V$ and apply the polytropic molar heat capacity formula.
solution_text:
The process is given by $P \propto V \implies P/V = \text{constant} \implies P V^{-1} = \text{constant}$.
Comparing this with the standard polytropic process equation $P V^n = \text{constant}$, we get $n = -1$.
The molar specific heat capacity for a polytropic process is:
$C = C_v + \frac{R}{1-n}$.
For a monatomic gas, $C_v = 1.5R$.
Substituting $C_v$ and $n = -1$:
$C = 1.5R + \frac{R}{1 - (-1)} = 1.5R + \frac{R}{2} = 2.0R$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Setting $n = 1$ instead of $n = -1$, which leads to a division by zero error, or using $n = 2$ incorrectly.

title: A refrigerator absorbs heat $Q_2$ from a cold reservoir at temperature $T_2$ and rejects heat $Q_1$ to a hot reservoir at temperature $T_1$, consuming work $W$. The coefficient of performance (COP) is given by:
notes: Use the definition of COP as heat extracted divided by work done.
solution_text:
The coefficient of performance (COP) $\beta$ of a refrigerator is the ratio of heat removed from the cold reservoir ($Q_2$) to the work input ($W$):
$\beta = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}$.
For a Carnot refrigerator, this ratio of heats equals the ratio of absolute temperatures: $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}$. Hence, the COP becomes:
$\beta = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Choosing the efficiency of a heat engine $\frac{T_1-T_2}{T_1}$ (option D) instead of the refrigerator COP.

title: The efficiency of a Carnot engine is $\eta$. To increase the efficiency of the engine, which of the following operations is more effective?

options.A: Decreasing the temperature of the sink ($T_{\text{sink}}$) by a temperature difference $\Delta T$
options.B: Increasing the temperature of the source ($T_{\text{source}}$) by the same temperature difference $\Delta T$
options.D: Increasing the volume expansion ratio of the cylinder

notes: Calculate and compare the efficiencies in both cases mathematically.
solution_text:
The efficiency of a Carnot engine is $\eta = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1}$, where $T_1$ is the source temperature and $T_2$ is the sink temperature.
Let's evaluate the change in efficiency:
- If we decrease the sink temperature by $\Delta T$:
$\eta_A = 1 - \frac{T_2 - \Delta T}{T_1} = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1} + \frac{\Delta T}{T_1} = \eta + \frac{\Delta T}{T_1}$.
- If we increase the source temperature by $\Delta T$:
$\eta_B = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1 + \Delta T}$.
Comparing the two, we have:
$\eta_A - \eta_B = \left(1 - \frac{T_2 - \Delta T}{T_1}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1 + \Delta T}\right) = \frac{T_2}{T_1 + \Delta T} - \frac{T_2 - \Delta T}{T_1} = \frac{\Delta T (T_1 - T_2 + \Delta T)}{T_1 (T_1 + \Delta T)}$.
Since $T_1 > T_2$, the numerator is positive, so $\eta_A > \eta_B$. Therefore, decreasing the sink temperature is more effective. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming they are equally effective because the absolute change in temperature $\Delta T$ is the same.

title: The net change in the entropy of the working substance during one complete cycle of a reversible Carnot engine is ________ $\text{J/K}$.
notes: Identify whether entropy is a path function or a state function.
solution_text: Entropy $S$ is a state function. For any cyclic process, since the final state of the working substance is identical to its initial state, the net change in its entropy must be zero: $\Delta S = S_f - S_i = 0$. For a reversible process, the entropy change of the universe (system + surroundings) is also zero.
common_mistake: Confusing the entropy change of the system with heat added divided by temperature ($Q_1/T_1$), yielding a non-zero value.

title: A Carnot engine having an efficiency of $40\%$ absorbs $1000\,\text{J}$ of heat from a hot reservoir. The amount of heat rejected by the engine to the cold reservoir is ________ $\text{J}$.
notes: Apply the efficiency formula $\eta = 1 - Q_{\text{out}}/Q_{\text{in}}$.
solution_text:
Efficiency $\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$, where $Q_{\text{in}}$ is the heat absorbed and $Q_{\text{out}}$ is the heat rejected.
Given $\eta = 0.40$ and $Q_{\text{in}} = 1000\,\text{J}$:
$0.40 = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{1000} \implies \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{1000} = 0.60 \implies Q_{\text{out}} = 600\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Equating the heat rejected to the work done ($400\,\text{J}$), neglecting that $Q_{\text{out}} = Q_{\text{in}} - W$.

title: A heat pump is used to maintain a room temperature at $27^\circ\text{C}$ while the outside temperature is $-3^\circ\text{C}$. The coefficient of performance (COP) of this heat pump, operating as a Carnot device, is:
notes: Use absolute temperatures and apply the Carnot heat pump COP formula $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = T_{\text{source}} / (T_{\text{source}} - T_{\text{sink}})$.
solution_text:
Temperatures: $T_{\text{sink}} = -3^\circ\text{C} = 270\,\text{K}$, $T_{\text{source}} = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300\,\text{K}$. The coefficient of performance (COP) of a Carnot heat pump is given by:
$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_{\text{source}}}{T_{\text{source}} - T_{\text{sink}}} = \frac{300}{300 - 270} = \frac{300}{30} = 10$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using the refrigerator COP formula which has $T_{\text{sink}}$ in the numerator, yielding 9 (option B).

title: Two Carnot engines A and B are operated in series. The first engine A receives heat at $900\,\text{K}$ and rejects to a reservoir at temperature $T$. The second engine B receives the heat rejected by the first engine and in turn rejects to a sink at $400\,\text{K}$. If the work outputs of the two engines are equal, the temperature $T$ is:
notes: Relate the temperature differences to work done, noting that heat quantities scale with absolute temperature.
solution_text:
Let the heat absorbed by engine A be $Q_1$ at $T_1 = 900\,\text{K}$, and rejected be $Q_2$ at $T$. The work done by engine A is $W_A = Q_1 - Q_2$.
Engine B receives heat $Q_2$ at $T$ and rejects $Q_3$ at $T_3 = 400\,\text{K}$. The work done by engine B is $W_B = Q_2 - Q_3$.
Since their work outputs are equal: $W_A = W_B \implies Q_1 - Q_2 = Q_2 - Q_3 \implies Q_1 + Q_3 = 2 Q_2$.
For Carnot engines: $\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T} = \frac{Q_3}{T_3} = k$. Substituting $Q = kT$:
$k T_1 + k T_3 = 2 k T \implies T = \frac{T_1 + T_3}{2}$.
Substituting $T_1 = 900\,\text{K}$ and $T_3 = 400\,\text{K}$:
$T = \frac{900 + 400}{2} = 650\,\text{K}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming the efficiencies are equal (which is the case for equal efficiency rather than equal work), which would yield the geometric mean $T = \sqrt{T_1 T_3} = 600\,\text{K}$ (option B).
